video.title: How Did Tom Dumoulin Win The 2017 Giro d'Italia ? | The GCN Show Ep. 229

all_candidates.title: How Did Tom Dumoulin Win The 2017 Giro d'Italia ? | The GCN Show Ep. 229

transcript.clean_text: - From Reykjavik, Iceland, welcome to GCN Show. - From Lake Moultrie, South Carolina, welcome to the GCN Show. - From Midwest Devo, welcome to the GCN Show. - Welcome to the GCN Show. - This week, we are taking a look back at the Giro Italia, one of the most exciting and closely-fought Grand Tours in living memory. Even in Matt's living memory. And, we try to analyse just how Tom Dumoulin won it. - Yeah, in tech of the week this week, we uncover a new type of bike, aero touring. Plus, we've got all of our usuals, as well. - It's good to be back, isn't it? - Yeah, it's been a while. - You can tell it's been a hard month, given how many haircuts are needed. You were right. We both need a trip. - I'm growing mine back. - Nice we brought in an extra mike for him, here. - What can you say about the 100th edition of the Giro Italia? It truly was an amazing race. In fact, one of the best all-time, I would hasten to add. Although, we'd probably need a little bit longer in the pub, wouldn't we, to actually nail down our top 5 in the Grand Tours. - Any excuse. - But it wasn't until the final week that things really started to move into top gear, when Dumoulin's rather healthy advantage went completely out the window. Let's face it, because of a quite poorly-timed poop. - It was terribly timed. Now, fortunately we were spared too many days of hypothetical questions about whether or not you would or would not take a dump in your shorts in order to win a Grand Tour. Because fortunately Dumoulin did end up prevailing. He managed to crawl back enough seconds in the final time trials over Horpino, Nibali, and Quintana to reclaim the Malia race. In fact, in doing that, he's the only rider in the history of cycling to go from fourth to first on the final day of the Grand Tour. - That's all well and good, but did you actually decide in that situation whether you would have just gone in your shorts in order to win your first Grand Tour? - I couldn't decide. Ultimately, Dumoulin's got age on his side. He could probably guarantee that he's gonna win another Grand Tour. But there is a precedent for peeing in your shorts. Just ask Greg LeMond. - There's been a lot of conjecture over the last few days. Should his rivals have waited when he jumped in the bushes and then dumped his shorts around his ankles? - Yeah, well. No, I think Tom Dumoulin himself said on Twitter that night that no, they should not have waited. Frankly, being able to resist the call of nature is a pretty important attribute to win a Grand Tour, isn't it? There are side effects to necking too many energy gels and too many energy drinks. We, in fact, have warned about such accidents in a video. - Even if you haven't experienced it directly yourself, you probably know someone who's had a severe stomach upset because of gel abuse. - Given that awful situation he found himself in, what are the actual secrets of Dumoulin's success? - I think we should start with his obvious strength which is time trialling. Although 69 kilometres of individual time trialling at the event this year, if you look at the time caps between him and his closest rivals, he put four minutes and 17 seconds into Nairo Quintana. Three minutes and a second into Vincenzo Nibali. And four minutes and eight seconds into Thibaut Pinot. He basically annihilated them over those two stages and then kind of held on to the climbs. - Held on seems a little bit harsh, mate. He did win-- - True. - Put 14 seconds in Quintana. And then, he only lost 24 seconds up to Blockhaus. To pick about it, just a little over a minute there. Then only 15 seconds on the last stage to Asiago. If you take all those into consideration, leaving aside Pugo stage just for one moment, which basically was the queen stage. Therefore, he may well have lost a little bit more time. But, it's only one minute and 34 that he conceded on the climbs. Going back to Stelvio, he was the fifth fastest man on that day. He did basically the whole thing on his own. He basically just rode a time trial, which is pretty clever if you ask me, if you're one of the world's best time trialers. - So, did he win because Quintana wasn't quite at his best? Not at 100%. I don't know if we should spend too much on speculating about a very difficult one to answer, but it does give us the opportunity to talk a little bit about Quintana. Of course, he was going for the Giro Tour Double this year. That's not going to happen. But, according to the siting statistician Killian Kelly, he's only the third person in history to be on the podium with three consecutive Grand Tours. The others being Bernard Hinault and Eddy Merckx, which makes you wonder, given how much he's already done back to back, whether the Giro-Tour double this year was a bit ambitious. - Well, maybe so, or just perhaps Quintana's second Grand Tour of the year is his best. - First one's like a leg opener. - Yeah, we'll obviously have to wait til the Tour de France where we will, of course, have an opportunity to make our infamous predictions. - Well, yeah. Speaking of which, this might be the real reason why Dumoulin actually won and Quintana finished second. - Pinot first, Vicenzo Nibali defending champion and Steven Kruijswijk. - Quintana for the win, Mikhal Landa and Vincenzo Nibali for third. - Mikhal Landa, Tom Dumoulin, and the winner will be Nairo Quintana. - Right, well I am taking that as a personal victory. The only person out of us three to predict Dumoulin in a top three at all. - Yeah, not a bad showing, all around. Two out of three, two out of three, one out of three. One of the others you got was fourth. - I'm still claiming a win. You can have second and you were last, out of us three. - I'm still on the podium. I still got third. - I'm like Nairo Quintana, I'm gonna improve towards the Tour de France. - Same here. We have some results for you now. It's competition time and we drew the winners of the pretty amazing physique competition with three lots of prizes up for grabs. We'll start with third place. The winner of the Fizik R1B shoes and bar tape is Daniel Rees. Congratulations, Daniel. A solid third place for you. The Vincenzo Nibali of our competition. - In second place, winning shoes, saddle, and bar tape, Cristof Ziel. Well done to you, congratulations. - Nairo Quintana for this week. - And finally, the winner of the R1B climbers' edition shoe, the saddle, and the bar tape is Ron Sloan, very good unto you. We'll be in touch and get those prizes to you ASAP. - You are gonna love those shoes, they are so cool. - It's now time for cycling shorts. - Let's start cycling shorts this week with great news for anybody that rides in California. California Department of Transport has announced a state-wide, integrated plan for cycling and pedestrians. Basically, double walking and triple cycling by 2020. Whilst at the same time, reduce fatalities by 10%, year on year. Some of the riding in California is absolutely fantastic. But, navigating urban areas can at times be a bit tricky. So, this should definitely help. - Yeah, that's a really good news story. There's been some news out of Aigle which is home of the UCI this week. They've announced the inaugural urban cycling world championship, stick with us. The Urban World Cycling Championships will consist of three disciplines. Now, rainbow jerseys up for grabs in each one. There's a mountain bike cross country eliminator race. There is a trials event and there's also a BMX freestyle event as well. It's gonna take place for the first time this coming November in Chengdu, in China. - All right, race news of a different kind this time. Very different kind, in fact. The TransAmerica Race, the TransAm, is starting next week. It's actually the fourth edition of this race across America. But, it is importantly very different from the Race Across America. - Yeah, very different indeed. Because this event is completely unsupported. They've got no support vehicles. There's no outside assistance allowed, no drafting. nothing really, just you against the elements. Incredibly, the record for this event was set by the late Mike Hall, just 17 and a half days. - Wow, it's like 4,200k's! - Well, thinking of Mike, the fifth edition of the European Transcontinental race will take place on July 28th, despite the tragic death of Hall earlier this year, of course one of the race's founders. - Any of you who have been into sport cycling or have a vague knowledge of it will be aware that the sport has had a deep problem with doping in years gone by. And actually, perhaps even now. There's two riders from Bardiani that were kicked off the Giro d'Italia before it even started. It's been incredibly sad and frustrating from inside the sport, hasn't it? However, on the outside, it appears that it's ripe for comedy because HBO have just released a trailer for their upcoming Tour de Pharmacy, which coincidentally comes out this July. It mocks and ridicules cycling's history. - Nearly every writer was on drugs. - Everyone was cheating, except for me. - How do you beat a man on drugs if you're not on drugs? - Wait, did you just admit to being on drugs? - Here we go. - Rightly so, would you say? - Well, personally, thinking I think rightly so. I must admit, I watched the trailer and did have quite a hearty chuckle at the cameo by a certain Lance Armstrong. Please check it out. - The diversification of road bikes continues apace in tech of the week this week. We have a new epic enduralgroad bike to show you and also aero touring. Which, before you all scoff disdainfully at, you need to see. It's frankly pretty amazing actually. - You have to wait a few moments though because first we're going to go through all road bikes because Cannondale last week, rather stealthily on their Facebook page, released a brand new bike with a super cross of force, SE. Although basically, it's the very standard cross bike with a few added components, which are more down the adventure and road lights. They've already done this in the past with their aluminium bike, the Crockett, as have Bayer with their terror. I guess it makes complete sense, really. Because cyclist frame sets are the keys of all purpose. But a complete cyclist build is rather niche. It makes sense for them to offer those frames to the public with a slightly different component to your specification. - Yeah, it does make sense. Especially if you know exactly what you want from the road bike. But, if you're new to the sport, it can be a nightmare. Just what sort of bike do you want? A road bike, a climbing bike, an aero bike, an endurance bike, a gravel bike, an adventure bike, a cyclo-cross bike, a time-trial bike, a triathlon bike, a touring bike? - Or, an aero touring bike? Oh, yes, we saw this little beauty on Instagram the other day. Rob English of English Cycles makes some of the most jaw-dropping beautiful custom bikes ever. In fact, we were lucky enough to see one of his time trial bikes in the raw at the spoke. And got to meet the man himself. But this latest creation definitely deserves a mention in the show, as well. He actually built it for himself to tackle the aforementioned TransAm race next week. In his own words, he wanted, and I quote, "The end goal is efficient speed." So, aerodynamics is important but it also needs to be comfortable for very long days in the saddle plus it will be able to carry a minimum amount of gear. - The result is, you can always see, are really quite stunning. But, as ever with English Cycles, the beauty is far more than just skin deep because the eye for attention on this bike really is quite incredible. Aero Parlee disc road covers, check. Custom aerodynamic saddle back, check that as well. Lightweight, you can also check that. Just 22 pounds in total, with all of those accessories on board. - That's nuts. - It's that time of the week again. It is hack, forward slash, bodge of the week. Already one bodge on the show. First up is a recycled high-jump stand hacked into a sprint work station. This came in from Stephen Browning. He said, "Could you please point out I am also using a chain keeper?" - Oh, matching colours. Blue, it's lovely. - It's nice. - Yeah, that is a really nice work station. Homemade stand. - Looks great, well-kept. - Chain keeper, even better. I was rubbish at the high jump. - Well, moving swiftly on from hacked work stands and chain keepers to this one, which I love, sent in by John Madrid. It is a homemade chain whip. But look at that, repurposing a crank arm. - It's actually quite arty, isn't it? - Yeah, brilliant. It's a definite hack. - Good photography as well. Next up, we have this from Mackenzie Cockram. As you can tell by my facial expression, this is the first time I've seen this photograph. There appears to be, from what I can see, a pair of handlebars with a stem facing backwards so it's a slammed inverse, reversed pair of bars. - I think it's for somebody who has a very short torso. - Looking at the way they've also slammed the saddle, it's pretty for somebody of short stature but quite interesting/dangerous. - Well, quite frankly, it's for someone that doesn't get around corners. There's not very much steering there, is there? - How's that gonna work? - Well, it's not, is it? - It's the wrong way round. - Genius, absolute genius. - The next one comes in from Edmund Solaiman. This came into this bike shop where he works, where somebody has managed to weld disc brake mounts on this old 27-inch tyre bite. Have a look at this. We've seen one of these welded on after market disc brakes things before, and it looks equally as dangerous. - It looks nice, but somebody should have reinforced the-- - Well, yeah, exactly. Forks are not designed to have that much pressure there. - See that, going to that one centimetre point. - Yeah, but apparently he did a 400k ride on it. It hasn't broken yet. - I hope there's no downhill section. - Which is good news. Right, then, moving on. We've got this one, which was spotted close to our GCN HQ. Man, there's a lot going on there. - It's no DeLorean, is it? - No, it's not. - Busy-looking bike. - It's probably got quite a bit of power to it, though. - Is that a lawnmower engine on the top, too? - I wonder if it's the kind of thing that you buy off Ebay. - A Victorian saddle. - It's admittedly highly illegal, but yeah, he's got a petrol engine strapped to his bike. - It looks remarkably aerodynamic though, doesn't it? - Right. Thanks, Adrian Stuttard, for sending that one in, genius. - Then we have this, over on Instagram from @ramulomir. It's look like it's-- - It's amazing. - It's adapted to the back of their car. - Styrofoam or something. - Yeah, it's basically sculpted to the Styrofoam at the shape of the bike. And the bikes can be in place. That is remarkable-- - I assume there's space for a pub underneath. - There's no space for people. Presumably it goes to races and event on its own. - That is very cool. He's got a pump there in back, as well. - Next up from @otolithe, he spotted this. It's springy but not particularly comfortable. - That's happened on my town bike. - You wouldn't sit on that without a pair of shorts on, would you? - I've got a removable leather cover on it now. Yeah, you always check and make sure it's still attached. Then, this one. That is a good bodge, actually. Look at that, fixed cleats. If you cleat brakes, that's how you fix it. What's it made out of? - A little piece of flattened steel, isn't it? - If you can find that-- - It's just another cleat. - I like that. That's a good hack. Or bodge. Well, whether it's a hack or a bodge, keep sending them on in to us in social media using the hashtag #GCNhack and we'll pick our favourites on both the hack and the bodge subjects this time next week. - Love them, absolutely love them. We've spoken about Tom Dumoulin already. But there were some other standout performances in the last week of the Giro. Take Vincenzo Nibali's queen stage finish in Bormeo. He was classy, he was entertaining, atypically, in fact, for himself. He couldn't drop Nairo Quintana on the climb to Stelvio, but he did so on the descent, then caught Mikel Landa and promptly outsprinted him at the finish to take the only Italian glory or stage win of the race. - I particularly like when he bunny-hopped over the standing water in the road. So he didn't get his tyres wet. That was very impressive. It was great to see Tejay van Garderen with BMC take a stage win after what's been a very tough couple of years for him. And likewise, Cannondale take a victory after two years without a Grand Tour stage win in the form of Pierre Roland. Understandably emotional with the team, after the race, as you will see he tweeted a week later on. And Mikel Landa deserved his stage after coming close on two previous occasions. Also, it was great to see Jos van Emden finally take a time trial away on the Grand Tour. He's been knocking on the door for years, finally got it on Sunday. Effectively, turned Milan a Dutch shade of orange With Dumoulin taking the victory overall, as well. - That's all right. But May isn't just about the Giro, is it? There's been loads of other really good races going on, including the Tour of Belgium, which was won by Jens Keukeleire. He's actually riding for the Belgian national team as opposed to his usual Scott outfit there. Second place was a particularly impressive performance from 21-year-old Quick-Step rider, Remi Cavagna from France. And, Tony Martin of Katusha-Alpecin was third. Then, very interestingly, Wout van Aert, world cycling cross champion finished tenth overall on GCN in what was a pretty special week for the cycling cross haters, as we're gonna see in just one moment. Before we leave racing news, we've got to talk about the Hammer series race. The first ever Hammer series race, which is coming up. But on Friday, in fact, it's a new, three-day, multi-discipline event format launched by Velon and it's attracted some of the best riders in the world, including probably an on-form, you might say, Tom Dumoulin. - Yeah, should be a cracking race. We've got some very exciting news of our own, in fact. Because you'll be able to watch all three days of the race live and for free right here on GCN with us commentating on the race, which we are extremely excited about. This week, we've got a very topical photo. We couldn't not put it in, could we really? Before then, lining up on the Sunday, on a mountain bike and finishing an incredible second place to Olympic and multi-time world champion Nino Schurter in world mountain bike cup. Even in Matt's living memory. And, we try to analyse just how Tom Dumoulin won it. - Yeah, in tech of the week this week, we uncover a new type of bike, aero touring. Plus, we've got

And then, if you're after some more content, well, why not click just over there for e-bike versus road bike? - Or, yeah it's cracking. A lot of fun doing that.
